findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the exact criteria-matching predicate to the `classify_pair` specification (e.g., 'a finding is in target-family iff `set(finding["criteria"]) & set(target_criteria)` is non-empty — set intersection, not equality') and add a golden-test fixture covering a finding with `criteria=['E4','G6']` against `target_criteria=['E4']` with the expected detection outcome stated.
evidence: Plan defines: 'a pure helper `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` applies: detected (mutant, recall): a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}`' — but never states the predicate by which a finding belongs to the 'target family'.

Per-finding `criteria` is a list confirmed from the gate output schema: `Returns {verdict: PASS|BLOCK|INDETERMINATE, ..., blocking[], advisory[], ...}` — adjudication corpus rows show multi-criteria findings like `{"criteria": ["COH", "E2", "F1"]}` (adjudication_corpus.jsonl:68).

The matching predicate choice is load-bearing: set-intersection (any(c in target_criteria for c in finding['criteria'])) vs. exact-equality vs. superset produces materially different recall numbers for multi-criteria findings.
ABSENCE: no matching predicate language ('set intersection', 'any overlap', 'exact match') appears anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: `classify_pair`'s criteria-matching predicate — the rule that decides whether a gate finding's `criteria` list belongs to the `target_criteria` family — is never specified, leaving the plan's entire detection-recall attribution mechanism under-defined for implementers.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; `harnesses/e2_counting.py` specification
scenarios: An implementer uses exact equality (`finding['criteria'] == target_criteria`) and silently under-counts detections for findings citing multiple criteria (e.g., a finding with criteria=['E4','G6'] is not attributed when target_criteria=['E4']).
Two independent implementers of `e2_counting.py` produce different recall numbers from the same verdict fixtures — the baseline table is non-reproducible.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE verdict handling section, add: 'A finding is **in target-family** iff `set(finding["criteria"]) & set(target_criteria)` is non-empty (set intersection — a multi-criteria finding is attributed to any class whose target set overlaps).'

findings.finding: classify_pair's criteria-matching rule (how a finding's criteria[] list is tested against target_criteria) is never defined, leaving the attribution logic — the plan's entire detection-recall mechanism — unspecified for implementers.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; e2_counting.py specification
findings.suggested_fix: In the classify_pair spec: 'target-family match = set(finding["criteria"]) & set(target_criteria) is non-empty (intersection — a finding citing E4+G6 is attributed to a class whose target_criteria includes E4)'. Add a fixture: finding with criteria=['E4','G1G2'], target_criteria=['E4'] → detected.

findings.finding: The plan describes `det_floor.py:345,392` as 'the DET-floor absence probe' that 'makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading the file: line 345 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'file', 'name': ref}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)` (P2 file-path resolution — checks whether a file a plan REFERENCES exists) and line 392 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'dependency', 'name': pkg}, ...)` (P3 package existence). Neither performs absent-capability probing or unnecessary-mechanism detection. Class-1 (absent asserted capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) are LLM-driven via E4, G1G2, G6, and the agentic verifier — not these DET-floor file/package probes.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) … makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'

findings.finding: No AC verifies that the gate actually received and processed the MUTATED plan text (i.e., that `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` routing to the clone is functioning). The isolation AC (AC 7) checks only that the real store's HEAD is unchanged after a run — it does not confirm that the mutant's modified description was the input to `review_plan`. A broken routing (e.g., `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` silently ignored, gate falling back to real store) would produce systematically low detection rates indistinguishable from 'the gate legitimately cannot catch this class,' silently producing an invalid baseline without triggering any AC failure.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 and AC 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s `run_validity` logic (or as a pre-flight check in `--self-check-isolation`): select the class-2 or class-3 smoke-test pair whose baseline detection is expected 'partial' (not near-zero), run it, and assert `mutant_verdict != control_verdict`. Record this check in `e2_run_validity.json` as `{routing_smoke_test: {mutant_verdict, control_verdict, routing_confirmed: bool}}`. Fail the run non-zero if `routing_confirmed` is False.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` in the harness environment block and in AC #4's prove command, so `ensure_fresh()` does not pull from the real repo's `origin` into the tmp clone and overwrite injected mutations before the gate reads them.
evidence: Plan states: `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` — only push suppression is specified; no mention of `REBAR_SYNC_PULL`
`src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39`: `_fresh(tracker)` calls `ticket_reads.ensure_fresh(tracker)` on every `show_ticket` call, not just on push
`src/rebar/_reads.py:14`: 'Opt out with `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (permanent alias `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`)'

Pre-existing plan-review cache corroborates: `.rebar/cache/plan-review/d125-b4ce-c57f-4f06/93a279adbd55403cb2be.json`, `cd410a962c8cccf8e143.json`, `e6542602f9619b0d32e6.json` all flag the missing REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off
Unverified capability: plan claims REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off achieves full isolation of the clone, but `ensure_fresh` pulls independently of push policy
findings.finding: The plan asserts `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` is sufficient for store isolation, but does not address `REBAR_SYNC_PULL`. The `_reads.show_ticket()` path calls `_fresh(tracker)` → `ensure_fresh(tracker)`, which performs a throttled `git fetch origin tickets` + reconverge on the tmp clone. Because the clone's `origin` is the real repo, an `ensure_fresh` triggered during a `review_plan()` call could overwrite injected mutations (committed into the clone by `rebar edit`) before the gate reads them, silently reverting the mutation and causing the mutant to be reviewed as its control.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph; `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH = 'off'` line in the harness code block; AC #4 (`run_e2_benchmark.py` runs via verified mechanism)
scenarios: After `rebar edit <id> --description=<mutant_text>` commits the injection, the first `review_plan()` call triggers `ensure_fresh()` → `git fetch origin tickets` + reconverge on the clone, potentially resetting the clone's tickets branch to match the real repo's HEAD, reverting the injected mutation before the gate reads it — the gate then reviews the unmodified control plan but records it as a mutant result

All 100+ gate runs appear to succeed, but the detection rates reported in `e2_baseline.json` are computed against un-mutated plans, making the baseline invalid (all classes look like ~0% detection, indistinguishable from the expected MISS-class pattern)
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness code block and in the mechanism description, set both: `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` and `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` (or equivalently `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`). Update AC #4's prove command to assert both vars are present in the run environment. Also update the README isolation section to document both directions of sync suppression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (or equivalently `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` in the `run_e2_benchmark.py` gate-execution environment block so `ensure_fresh` is suppressed and the tmp clone's ticket state remains frozen to its initial post-injection snapshot for the full benchmark run.
evidence: Plan specifies: `os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PUSH'] = 'off'` but never mentions `REBAR_SYNC_PULL`
`_reads.py:37-39`: `def _fresh(tracker): ticket_reads.ensure_fresh(tracker)` — called unconditionally in `show_ticket` and `list_tickets`
`_engine_support/reads.py:186-251`: `ensure_fresh` runs `git fetch origin tickets` on the tracker directory unless `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) is set
The tmp clone's `origin` = the real repo checkout, so fetches are real disk I/O operations and can advance the clone's remote-tracking refs during a benchmark run
ABSENCE: plan text contains no mention of `REBAR_SYNC_PULL` or `REBAR_NO_SYNC`
findings.finding: The gate-execution mechanism sets `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` but omits `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off`, so every `review_plan` call on a mutant/control triggers `ensure_fresh()` → `git fetch origin tickets` on the tmp clone (whose `origin` points back to the real repo). This means each benchmark gate run makes a real network/disk fetch against the real repo, and the clone can diverge from its initial state if the real `tickets` branch advances mid-run — introducing non-reproducibility in the mutant store state.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism (verified) / `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; `src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39` (`ensure_fresh` called on every `show_ticket`/`list_tickets`)
scenarios: During a long benchmark run the real `tickets` branch advances; `ensure_fresh` merges the new commits into the tmp clone, silently altering the mutant store's ticket state mid-run and making results non-reproducible.
The benchmark runs slower than expected because each of N mutant+control gate calls (≥100 calls) triggers a throttled-but-real `git fetch` on the clone.
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness's gate-execution environment setup, add `os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PULL'] = 'off'` (or `os.environ['REBAR_NO_SYNC'] = '1'`) alongside `os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PUSH'] = 'off'`. Update the AC's `review_plan` invocation description to include this env var. This is a one-line addition that makes the isolation complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` (or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) to the harness environment setup alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`, and state explicitly in the gate-execution mechanism paragraph that both push and pull sync are suppressed for the duration of the benchmark run.
evidence: Plan specifies: `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` — but no `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
Confirmed in `src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39`: every `show_ticket` call invokes `_fresh(tracker)` → `ensure_fresh(tracker)`.
Confirmed in `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:186-251`: `ensure_fresh` runs a throttled `git fetch origin tickets` on the tracker dir unless `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1` is set.

Because the mutant clone is created with `git clone --local --branch tickets`, its `origin` points to the real repo and `origin/tickets` is a valid remote-tracking ref — fetch will succeed and could reconverge the clone's branch before or during injection.
findings.finding: The gate-execution mechanism sets `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` to prevent outbound writes but does not suppress the inbound pull path (`REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off`), leaving `ensure_fresh()` able to `git fetch origin tickets` on the tmp mutant clone during each `review_plan()` call — a processing sub-step with no specified fallback or suppression.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism (verified) section; ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` bullet
scenarios: Between clone creation and the first `rebar edit` injection, a `review_plan()` call triggers `ensure_fresh()` which fetches and reconverges the clone's `tickets` branch to real-store HEAD — potentially overwriting the intended mutant state.

Even after injection (the local branch is ahead so reconverge is safe), the fetch adds network latency and git subprocess overhead to every gate call in the ≥100-invocation benchmark run, without benefit.
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-execution env block, add `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` next to `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`. Document: 'both REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off and REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off are set so no git fetch or push touches the real store or the clone's remote-tracking refs during any review_plan() call.'

findings.finding: The crash-resumable behavior of `run_e2_benchmark.py` ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (`e2_verdicts.jsonl`, crash-resumable)') is a described deliverable in Scope with no covering AC. No prove-command or test asserts that a partial `e2_verdicts.jsonl` allows the harness to skip already-completed rows on re-run.
findings.location: Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC4, AC5, AC6, AC7 (none covers crash-resume)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a grep (or explicit scope statement) to the README AC that verifies the counting-rule contract is documented — e.g., `grep -qE "advisory.*detect|INDETERMINATE.*FN" docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md && echo COUNTING-RULE-OK` — so the classify_pair semantics (advisory=detected, INDETERMINATE=FN) are guaranteed to appear in the E2 writeup section.
evidence: AC grep: `grep -q "run-validity" ... && grep -Eq "degrad"` — verifies two keywords but not the counting-rule contract
Plan states: 'advisory counts, because the proposed criteria ship advisory-first' and 'INDETERMINATE ⇒ gate did not act: ... counts as NOT DETECTED (FN)' — critical for cross-R-story comparability
ABSENCE: no AC or README scope bullet explicitly requires documenting the classify_pair counting rules in README
findings.finding: The README AC's grep patterns (`grep -q "run-validity"`, `grep -Eq "degrad"`) verify structural keywords but do NOT check that the counting-rule contract (advisory findings count as detected; INDETERMINATE ⇒ FN on mutants, non-FP on controls; block-only counts as FP) is documented. This contract is the most load-bearing detail for R1/R2/R3/R4 implementers who re-run the harness and interpret their detection numbers — if it is absent or ambiguous in the README, downstream R-stories may miscount results.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — last AC bullet (README.md prove command)
scenarios: An R3 implementer reads the README to understand how to interpret their benchmark output but finds only a description of the harness mechanics; they independently decide advisory=not-detected (the opposite of the spec), producing an incomparable detection number.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the README scope bullet to include: 'counting-rule contract (advisory findings count as detected; INDETERMINATE ⇒ FN on mutants; block-only ⇒ FP on controls)'. Add a corresponding grep to the README AC prove command.

findings.finding: The AC1 proving command does not verify presence of `source_ticket_id`, `children`, `mutation_op`, or `mutation_diff` fields in corpus rows, even though the AC text explicitly requires 'every row carrying' all seven named fields (plus `mutation_op`/`mutation_diff` for mutants). The script only accesses `r['defect_class']` and `r['role']` universally, and `r['plan_text']` only for class-5 rows — rows missing the other required fields would pass silently.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — first AC (corpus structure proving command)
findings.suggested_fix:
Insert after the existing Counter assertions:
```python
req_all = {'pair_id','defect_class','role','source_ticket_id','plan_text','children'}
req_mut = {'mutation_op','mutation_diff'}
assert all(req_all <= set(r) for r in rows), [r for r in rows if not req_all <= set(r)][:3]
assert all(req_mut <= set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant'), [r for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant' and not req_mut <= set(r)][:3]
assert all({'role'} <= set(r) and r['role'] in {'mutant','control'} for r in rows)
```

evidence: description is 28368 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` (cross-checks every selected base ticket's corpus row against the 5-condition filter; exits 0 on full pass, exits non-zero naming the first violating ticket) to the Scope description of `assemble_e2_mutants.py` so the CLI contract matches AC2's proving command.
evidence: Scope says only: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing'
AC2 proving command: `python3 … assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK`
Behavior description ('exits 0 only if every selected base ticket's corpus row passes the 5-condition filter; non-zero names the first violating ticket') appears ONLY in the AC prose, not in Scope
findings.finding: The proving command in AC2 invokes `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`, but the Scope section only describes the `--dry-run` flag for that script ('`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing'). The `--verify-clean` flag — which must exit non-zero and name the first violating ticket — is never specified as a CLI interface requirement in the Scope. An executor implementing the script from the Scope description may omit this flag, causing the AC2 proving command to fail with an argparse error.
findings.location: ## Scope — `assemble_e2_mutants.py` bullet; AC2 proving command
scenarios: Executor implements only `--dry-run` as specified in Scope; `--verify-clean` is unrecognized; AC2 command exits with argparse error rather than verifying the filter.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `assemble_e2_mutants.py` Scope bullet: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing; `--verify-clean` (combinable with `--dry-run`) re-reads each selected base ticket's `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row, asserts all 5 filter conditions, and exits non-zero naming the first violating ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--self-check-isolation` to the Scope description of `run_e2_benchmark.py`, specifying: asserts `os.path.isdir(<TMP_MUTANT_STORE>/.git)` before any injection, runs one representative mutant/control pair through the gate, writes `e2_run_validity.json`, and exits; used exclusively by the isolation AC proving command.
evidence: Scope describes `run_e2_benchmark.py` behavior in detail but never lists `--self-check-isolation` as a CLI argument
AC7 proving command: `.venv/bin/python … run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`

The flag's behavior is alluded to in `## What`: 'a mutant gate run leaves the real `.tickets-tracker` branch HEAD unchanged' and 'harness ASSERTS `os.path.isdir(…/.git)` BEFORE any injection' — but the CLI entry point is unspecified
findings.finding: The proving command in AC7 invokes `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`, but the Scope section's description of `run_e2_benchmark.py` does not list `--self-check-isolation` as a supported CLI flag. The flag's behavior (assert `.git` is a directory, run a single-pair gate run, then exit) is described only in the `## What` section's store-isolation paragraph and in the AC7 prose — not as a Scope-level script-interface requirement. An executor may not implement this flag.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` bullet; AC7 proving command
scenarios: Executor implements `run_e2_benchmark.py` with only the main-run and `emit_baseline` seam as described in Scope; `--self-check-isolation` is unrecognized; AC7 command fails with argparse error; isolation guarantee is never validated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` Scope bullet: '`--self-check-isolation` mode: creates the tmp mutant store clone, asserts `os.path.isdir(<clone>/.git)` (aborts non-zero if the check fails), runs the gate on one representative pair, writes `e2_run_validity.json`, and exits — used by AC7 to prove isolation without a full benchmark run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the harness description (and/or the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope bullet) that it reads `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and passes it explicitly to `run_validity(verdicts, indeterminate_cap=t['indeterminate_cap'])` — so the JSON-stored cap is the authoritative source and the Python default is only a fallback guard.
evidence: "run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)" — 0.20 as a Python default
"'indeterminate_cap' in t" — AC's JSON-structure assertion implies the cap is committed to e2_thresholds.json
Harness section says 'Reads its targets from runs/e2_thresholds.json' without specifying that the cap is among the values read and forwarded to run_validity
findings.finding: The `run_validity` helper exposes `indeterminate_cap=0.20` as a Python default parameter, but the plan says `e2_thresholds.json` contains `indeterminate_cap` AND that the harness 'Reads its targets from `runs/e2_thresholds.json`'. The harness description never explicitly states it reads and passes the JSON-stored cap to `run_validity`. If `run_e2_benchmark.py` calls `run_validity(verdicts)` without forwarding the JSON value, the committed threshold in `e2_thresholds.json` is silently shadowed by the hardcoded default — making the cap non-configurable in practice despite appearing configurable.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; `harnesses/e2_counting.py` signature; `runs/e2_thresholds.json` AC
scenarios: A future R-story re-runs the harness after updating `indeterminate_cap` in `e2_thresholds.json` to 0.15 (tighter), but the harness silently uses 0.20 because `run_validity` was called without the argument — the cap change has no effect and the validity guard is weaker than intended.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## Scope bullet for `run_e2_benchmark.py`, add: 'reads `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and passes it explicitly as `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=thresholds["indeterminate_cap"])`.' Update the `run_validity` signature note to clarify the default is only a safety fallback, not the operative value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope bullet (or the INDETERMINATE-handling section) that the harness reads `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and forwards it explicitly: `run_validity(verdicts, indeterminate_cap=t['indeterminate_cap'])` — making the JSON the sole authoritative source.
evidence: Plan states: `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` — hardcoded default in the helper signature.
AC3 proof command asserts `'indeterminate_cap' in t` — confirming the cap is committed to `e2_thresholds.json`.
Plan states harness 'Reads its targets from `runs/e2_thresholds.json`' — but this phrase appears in the context of detection/FP targets, not the INDETERMINATE cap.
ABSENCE: no plan statement explicitly says the harness reads `t['indeterminate_cap']` from the JSON and passes it as `run_validity(verdicts, indeterminate_cap=t['indeterminate_cap'])`.
findings.finding: The harness spec says it 'Reads its targets from `runs/e2_thresholds.json`' but never specifies that it reads `indeterminate_cap` from that JSON and passes it explicitly to `run_validity` — leaving the authoritative cap source ambiguous between the JSON-committed value and the Python default `indeterminate_cap=0.20`.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` bullet; `harnesses/e2_counting.py` signature `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)`
scenarios: A future R-story tightens `indeterminate_cap` in `e2_thresholds.json` to 0.15; the harness silently uses 0.20 (the Python default) because `run_validity` was called without forwarding the argument — the cap change has no effect and the validity guard is weaker than intended.
The committed `e2_thresholds.json` and the Python default diverge over time, creating a silent inconsistency that only manifests when the cap is intentionally changed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope bullet: 'reads `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and passes it explicitly to `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=t["indeterminate_cap"])` so the committed threshold is the sole authoritative source.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-run cost/token estimate and API rate-limit / throttle / backoff handling to `run_e2_benchmark.py` (e.g., configurable inter-call sleep or Anthropic retry-with-backoff), and document the estimated $/run in the README.
evidence: Plan states: 'per mutant/control runs the CURRENT gate via the verified mechanism … review_plan(ticket_id, source="local", …)'
Plan specifies ≥10 mutant + 1 control per class × 5 classes = ≥100 invocations
No mention of rate-limit handling, retry/backoff, or $/run estimate anywhere in ## What, ## Scope, or Acceptance Criteria
Rubric: 'an O(n) LLM-calls-per-item pattern is MAJOR'
findings.finding: The benchmark harness issues O(n) LLM/gate calls — ≥100 production `review_plan` invocations per full run (5 classes × ≥10 mutants + 10 controls) — with no per-run cost estimate, no API rate-limit / backoff / throttling logic, and no token-budget cap stated anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: A full benchmark run hits the Anthropic API rate limit mid-batch and aborts at pair 60/100, wasting all prior spend with no throttling to prevent it
No budget estimate means the team cannot forecast cost before authorizing the run
findings.suggested_fix: Add `time.sleep(INTER_CALL_DELAY)` (configurable, default ~1 s) between `review_plan` calls; document that at ≥100 calls × estimated token cost the run costs approximately $X; use the Anthropic SDK's built-in retry/backoff for transient 429s.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `run_e2_benchmark.py` handles `rebar edit` injection failures: define whether the harness aborts with a non-zero exit and removes the partial clone, skips and logs the failed mutant (with a missing-mutant count check), or retries — and extend the crash-resume spec to cover the injection phase, not only the verdict-journaling phase.
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — crash-resume is specified only for the verdict-output phase
'injects each seeded defect into the clone via rebar edit <id> --description=…' — injection step has no stated failure mode or partial-state recovery
The harness ASSERTS clone is standalone before injection, but no equivalent guard is specified for injection-step failures mid-run
findings.finding: The harness specifies crash-resumable journaling for the verdict-output phase (`e2_verdicts.jsonl`) but does not specify error handling for the earlier mutant-injection phase: if `rebar edit <id> --description=…` fails for a given mutant (e.g., the ticket id is not found in the clone, the edit command times out, or a git commit fails mid-injection), the plan does not define whether the harness skips that mutant, aborts the run, retries, or leaves the clone in a partially-mutated state. A partially-mutated clone could cause subsequent gate runs to operate on an inconsistently mutated ticket, silently producing a verdict attributed to the wrong mutant.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism / ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: A researcher runs the benchmark on a corpus where one ticket's description edit exceeds a field-length limit or triggers a validation error in rebar edit; the harness has no defined behavior, and the clone may be left in a partial state where some mutants are injected and others are not, making the resulting e2_verdicts.jsonl corpus internally inconsistent without any error surfaced to the operator.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an injection-phase guard: wrap each `rebar edit` call in a try/except, log failures to a `e2_injection_errors.jsonl` file, abort the run (exit non-zero, delete or quarantine the partial clone) if any injection fails, and document the resume path as 'fix the cause and re-run from scratch on a fresh clone' — making injection failures loud rather than silent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm the `tests/unit/experiments/` subdirectory will be created (and add it explicitly to Scope), or align the test file paths with the existing flat `tests/unit/` convention used by all comparable experiment tests.
evidence: ABSENCE: `tests/unit/experiments/` directory does not exist in the repo
`test_gate_eval_classifier.py` (the closest analogue — a CI-collectable test for an experiment harness) lives at `tests/unit/test_gate_eval_classifier.py`, not under any `experiments/` subdirectory
No existing test file in `tests/unit/` uses a nested `experiments/` path
findings.finding: The plan places new test files at `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` and `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py`, but the subdirectory `tests/unit/experiments/` does not exist in the repository. All existing experiment-related unit tests (e.g., `test_gate_eval_classifier.py`) live directly under `tests/unit/`, not in a nested `experiments/` subdirectory. The plan does not enumerate creating this new directory.
findings.location: ## Scope — last two bullet points: `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` and `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py`
scenarios: CI discovery (`pytest tests/unit/`) will fail to collect the new tests unless the directory is explicitly created (with an `__init__.py` if required by the test runner config), or the path is wrong and the tests land in a location inconsistent with the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit pull-sync suppression for the mutant clone (e.g. inject `sync.pull = 'off'` into the clone's `.rebar/config.toml` or write the `/tmp/.ticket-sync-<md5>` throttle marker to its path before any gate run), since `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` suppresses push only and `ensure_fresh` will attempt `git fetch` + reconverge on the first read of a fresh clone.
evidence: Plan states: 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off' prevents contamination, but this env-var only suppresses _store/push.py's push path.
reads.py:197: `if no_sync or _sync_disabled(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))): return` — pull suppression requires sync.pull='off' in config, not REBAR_SYNC_PUSH.
_engine_support/reads.py:225: `if marker_age < 60: return` — throttle is skipped on a fresh clone with no marker file.
_store/sync.py:165: `if not _ok(tracker, 'fetch', remote_name, branch, '--quiet'): return` — fetch runs on every reconverge that clears the throttle.
findings.finding: The harness's clone-isolation mechanism is vulnerable to ensure_fresh auto-reconverge overwriting injected mutation commits: every _reads.show_ticket() call triggers ensure_fresh(), which skips the throttle on a fresh clone (no /tmp/.ticket-sync-<md5> marker exists yet) and performs git fetch origin tickets + reconverge. REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off (the only suppression the plan cites) disables push only (_store/push.py), not pull. Pull suppression requires sync.pull='off' in the config read from the clone's parent directory (reads.py:197: _sync_disabled checks load_config(os.path.dirname(realpath(tracker))).sync.pull). In the normal sequential case (local strictly ahead) reconverge is a no-op, but if any concurrent commit lands on the real tickets branch between the git clone and the first gate read, origin/tickets fetches the new SHA, the clone diverges, and _union_merge runs — potentially merging unintended real commits into the mutant store before review_plan reads the ticket.
findings.location: ## What — 'Gate-execution mechanism (verified)' section and 'Mutant store = an INDEPENDENT git clone' paragraph
scenarios: A concurrent rebar write to the real tickets branch (e.g. another ticket edited during a long benchmark run) advances origin/tickets; the clone's first ensure_fresh fetches it; origin is no longer an ancestor of clone HEAD; _union_merge runs and injects the real commit into the mutant store before review_plan reads the ticket.

Even in the single-writer case, ensure_fresh fires on every first-read-per-clone (no throttle marker), performs a git fetch from origin (the real repo), and — if origin/tickets HEAD == clone base (pre-mutation) but the reconverge wrongly identifies local as not strictly ahead due to a timing edge — could reset the clone to the pre-mutation state.
findings.suggested_fix: After `git clone --local --branch tickets REAL_REPO TMP_MUTANT_STORE`, either: (a) write a fresh throttle marker at `/tmp/.ticket-sync-{hashlib.md5(os.path.realpath(TMP_MUTANT_STORE).encode()).hexdigest()[:12]}` with the current epoch to suppress auto-sync for 60 s per run, or (b) write `[sync]\npull = "off"` into `os.path.dirname(TMP_MUTANT_STORE)/.rebar/config.toml` (the path `_sync_disabled` reads) before any ticket read. The harness's `--self-check-isolation` AC should also assert the throttle marker or config is in place before the first gate call.

findings.finding: The mutation-assembly script (`assemble_e2_mutants.py`) has no CI-collectable test asserting that its per-class mechanical transformations are semantically correct (e.g., that a class-2 mutation produces layer-partitioned children rather than merely differently-named ones, that a class-3 mutation actually removes a `depends_on`/`blocks` edge, that a class-4 mutation inserts an unnecessary mechanism rather than any arbitrary change). The only structural validation of the mutants is the corpus-level prove command (which checks schema fields and class-5 tag presence/absence) and the `--dry-run --verify-clean` flag (which validates the cleanly-closed filter, not the mutations). A bug in any of these transformations would silently produce an invalid corpus — one that passes all structural ACs but measures something other than the intended defect class — invalidating the downstream R-row benchmarks.
findings.location: ## Scope — `assemble_e2_mutants.py`; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 (corpus prove command)
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the per-class mutation logic from `assemble_e2_mutants.py` into a pure function per class (e.g., `mutate_class2(base_plan, children) -> mutated_children`) with no I/O dependency, then write golden tests in `test_e2_assembly.py` using small synthetic base-plan dicts. The class-5 pattern (tag-presence assertion in the prove command) already demonstrates the approach — replicate it as importable tests for all five classes.

findings.finding: The mutation-assembly logic in `assemble_e2_mutants.py` for defect classes 1–4 has no per-class construct-validity test: AC 1's prove command only checks row counts, pairing, required fields, and the class-5 tag strip — it does NOT assert that class-1 mutations actually introduce an absent-capability assertion, that class-2 produces layer-partitioned (not vertically-sliced) children, that class-3 names an artifact without a `depends_on`/`blocks` edge, or that class-4 introduces an unnecessary mechanism. If the mutation logic is subtly wrong for any of these classes, the baseline silently measures the wrong defect, invalidating downstream R1/R3/R4 benchmarks.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 prove command; `assemble_e2_mutants.py` scope entry; defect classes 1–4 mutation definitions in ## What
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean` (AC 2) or add a dedicated `--verify-mutations` flag that, for each committed mutant row, runs a per-class semantic check function and exits non-zero naming the first failing pair. Mirror AC 1's class-5 prove pattern: for class 3, assert `any(edge in control_children for edge in ['depends_on','blocks']) and not any(edge in mutant_children ...)` etc. Add the four class checks to `test_e2_counting.py` or a new `test_e2_mutations.py` using synthetic corpus rows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the crash-resume skip mechanism explicitly: on startup, `run_e2_benchmark.py` reads already-journaled `pair_id`s from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` and skips those pairs; or truncates the file and restarts cleanly (with the truncate behavior documented).
evidence: Plan says: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)'
No specification of how a resumed run detects already-completed pair_ids in the journal
AC4 proof reads all rows: if duplicates exist, `n_mutants`/`detected` counts are overcounted
ABSENCE: no 'skip if pair_id already in journal' logic described anywhere
findings.finding: The plan asserts `e2_verdicts.jsonl` is 'crash-resumable' but does not define the skip-already-completed-pairs mechanism — on a restart the harness will either re-run all pairs from scratch (doubling LLM spend) or silently append duplicate rows (corrupting the baseline count and detection rates).
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py; Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (BASELINE-OK proof)
scenarios: Run crashes at pair 47; restart re-runs all 100 pairs; `e2_verdicts.jsonl` accumulates 147 rows; `e2_baseline.json` detection_rate is computed on the duplicate-inflated set, silently producing wrong numbers that pass the AC4 proof
Combined with the LLM cost issue, a crash-and-restart could double the API spend
findings.suggested_fix: On startup, load already-journaled pair_ids from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` (if it exists) into a set; skip any pair whose id is already present before issuing a `review_plan` call. Document this in the README under 'crash recovery'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify per-pair exception handling in `run_e2_benchmark.py`: catch exceptions per pair, log a structured error row to `e2_verdicts.jsonl` (with `verdict: ERROR`), and define whether the run continues (skip+log) or aborts; ensure `e2_run_validity.json` is always written even on partial runs.
evidence: Plan specifies the happy-path gate call: 'review_plan(ticket_id, source="local", repo_root=REAL_REPO, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)'
ABSENCE: no try/except, error-logging, or skip-and-continue logic described for individual pair failures
`run_validity` guard checks INDETERMINATE verdicts but not exception-aborts — an exception leaves no verdict row at all
findings.finding: Per-pair exception handling in the benchmark harness is unspecified: if `review_plan()` raises an exception (network error, context-length error, unexpected crash) on a single pair, the harness behavior — abort entire run, skip+log, or retry — is not defined, leaving the run in an undetermined partial state.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py; ## What — Gate-execution mechanism
scenarios: One malformed mutant plan causes a context-length exception; the harness aborts; `e2_verdicts.jsonl` is partial; `e2_run_validity.json` is not written (run_validity never ran); the AC4 proof fails with a confusing error rather than a clear diagnostic
A transient network error on pair 3/100 aborts an otherwise valid run
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `review_plan(...)` call in `try/except Exception as exc` and journal `{pair_id, verdict: 'ERROR', error: str(exc)}`; treat ERROR rows as INDETERMINATE for counting purposes (already the strict/safe choice per the plan's own INDETERMINATE rule); always write `e2_run_validity.json` in a `finally` block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup path for the tmp mutant store: use `tempfile.mkdtemp()` (random path, no reuse risk) and wrap the run in a `try/finally: shutil.rmtree(tmp_path, ignore_errors=True)` so the clone is removed on both success and failure.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>'
ABSENCE: no `shutil.rmtree(TMP_MUTANT_STORE)` or `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` / context-manager cleanup anywhere in ## Scope or ACs
ACs prove isolation (HEAD unchanged) but do not assert the tmp dir is removed after the run
findings.finding: The tmp mutant store (a standalone git clone created per run) has no specified RETIRE/cleanup path — the plan defines CREATE but not DELETE, so tmp clones accumulate on disk after each run (success or failure).
findings.location: ## What — Mutant store paragraph; ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: Each benchmark run (including aborted ones) leaves a full git clone (~hundreds of MB for a real ticket store) in a tmp location; multiple runs accumulate unbounded disk usage
A crash before cleanup leaves a stale clone that a subsequent run may incorrectly reuse if TMP path is deterministic
findings.suggested_fix: Use `with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp_mutant_store:` around the entire harness run body so cleanup is guaranteed even on crash; document this in the README.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Write `e2_baseline.json` and `e2_run_validity.json` atomically: write to a `.tmp` sibling file, then `os.replace(tmp_path, final_path)` — so readers always see either the previous complete file or the new complete file, never a partial write.
evidence: Plan specifies 'emits `e2_baseline.json` ONLY when every class passed the validity guard' and 'ALWAYS writes `e2_run_validity.json`'
ABSENCE: no write-to-tmp-then-rename (atomic replace) pattern mentioned
AC4 proof does `json.load(open('…/e2_baseline.json'))` — a partial write produces a JSONDecodeError that masks the underlying cause
findings.finding: `e2_baseline.json` and `e2_run_validity.json` are written directly to their final paths without atomic rename — a crash or signal during the write produces truncated/corrupt JSON that silently fails the AC proof on subsequent runs.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py; Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (BASELINE-OK proof)
scenarios: OOM-kill or SIGKILL mid-write to `e2_baseline.json` leaves a truncated file; next AC4 proof run fails with JSONDecodeError rather than a meaningful validity error; the guard logic (`emit_baseline` returns non-zero) is irrelevant because the file is already partially written
findings.suggested_fix:
```python
import os, json, tempfile
def atomic_write_json(path, data):
    dir_ = os.path.dirname(path)
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w', dir=dir_, delete=False, suffix='.tmp') as f:
        json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
        tmp = f.name
    os.replace(tmp, path)
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `passes.py:269,514` with the correct passes.py locations of the agentic plan-review code-reading paths (e.g., the AGENT-tier dispatch in the workflow, or verify via `resolve_code_root` cascade in `orchestrator.py`) and verify that `source='local', repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT` routes code-grounding to the real checkout.
evidence: passes.py line 269: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — inside `_resolve_system`, a prompt resolver, not an agentic finder
passes.py line 514: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — inside `summarize_for_isf`, an ISF context summarizer
The actual agentic finders are AGENT-tier criteria dispatched by the workflow engine, not at these passes.py line numbers

The plan rests on this citation to justify that `source='local', repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT` causes code-grounding to read the real modules — the claim may be true via other paths (cfg.repo_path in resolve_code_root), but the specific line-number evidence is wrong
findings.finding: The plan's gate-run mechanism section asserts 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules'. The `passes.py:269,514` citations are wrong: line 269 is `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_resolve_system` (prompt resolver), and line 514 is `prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)` inside `summarize_for_isf` (ISF context summarizer). Neither is an 'agentic finder reading the REAL modules'. The actual DET-floor absence probes are in `det_floor.py` (lines 345, 392), already correctly cited. The plan uses the wrong line numbers for `passes.py` to justify that code-grounding reads the real repo.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: '…the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules…'
scenarios: A reviewer following the cited lines to verify code-grounding behavior will find prompt resolution code, not agentic code-readers, creating a traceability gap
If the actual agentic code-reading path does NOT flow through cfg.repo_path correctly in the harness scenario, the verification shortfall goes undetected because the cited evidence is wrong
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the citation with the correct mechanism: `orchestrator.py:195` (`resolve_code_root(repo_root, cfg_repo_path=cfg.repo_path, allow_checkout_fallback=False)`) and the AGENT-tier criteria dispatch in `gate_dispatch.py` / `production_batch_runner.py` — these are the actual paths through which code-grounding reads the real modules when `repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT` is passed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the agentic-finder line citations from `passes.py:269,514` to the actual agentic-tier entry point (`pass1_chunk` at `passes.py:281` with `agentic=True`) in the gate-execution mechanism paragraph.
evidence: passes.py:269 contains `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_resolve_system` — a prompt resolver, not an agentic finder
passes.py:514 contains `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)` inside `summarize_for_isf` — an ISF log summarizer, not an agentic finder
The actual agentic finder is `pass1_chunk` at line 281, called with `agentic=True` for the AGENT-tier criteria
findings.finding: The plan cites `passes.py:269,514` as the location of the 'agentic finders' that 'read the REAL modules' for class-1 and class-4 detection. Line 269 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (a prompt-resolution call) and line 514 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)` (an ISF summarizer prompt call). Neither is an agentic finder. The agentic finding path is `pass1_chunk` (line 281+) with `agentic=True`, which executes the code-grounding tier.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: 'the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules'
scenarios: A developer implementing `run_e2_benchmark.py` who tries to verify agentic code-grounding by looking at the cited line numbers will find unrelated prompt-resolution code, not the grounding path, potentially leading them to misunderstand which gate path is exercised for classes 1 and 4.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules' with 'the agentic-tier finder (`passes.py:281` — `pass1_chunk` with `agentic=True`) reads the REAL modules via the code-grounding oracle'.

findings.finding: The mutation procedure for defect class 4 ('unnecessary mechanism') is underspecified as a mechanical operation. It is defined only as 'a change the plan does not need (FixedBench over-action)' — 'FixedBench' is not defined anywhere in this plan, and no concrete example is given of WHAT change to inject (e.g., 'add a dependency on a new library', 'add a new background job', 'add a caching layer'). An executor must independently decide what constitutes an 'unnecessary mechanism' to seed, which is a judgment call rather than a mechanical mutation.
findings.location: ## What — defect class definitions, class 4

findings.finding: The plan cites `passes.py:269,514` as the 'agentic finders' that 'read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading those lines: line 269 is inside `_resolve_system()` (a prompt resolver calling `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)`), and line 514 is inside `summarize_for_isf()` (an ISF session-log summarizer). Neither is an agentic finder. The actual agentic Pass-1 finder is `pass1_chunk()` with `execution_mode='agentic'` at approximately line 314, and the container-agentic path is around lines 406/439.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: 'the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(passes.py:269,514)' with '(passes.py:281-314 for pass1_chunk agentic mode; passes.py:398-440 for the container-agentic path)' or remove the specific line citations and reference the function names directly.

findings.finding: The plan cites `passes.py:269,514` as where 'the DET-floor absence probe and the agentic finders read the REAL modules', but these line numbers are incorrect. Line 269 is inside `_resolve_system` (a `prompts.get_prompt()` call), and line 514 is the first line of `summarize_for_isf` — a non-agentic single-turn ISF summarizer, not an agentic finder. The actual agentic execution_mode assignments are at lines 314 and 406 in passes.py. An implementer verifying the cited mechanism would find the wrong code.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: '`passes.py:269,514`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`)' with 'agentic finders (`passes.py:314,406`)' in the gate-execution mechanism paragraph.

findings.finding: The plan claims '`passes.py:269,514`' is where 'agentic finders read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading `passes.py` at those exact lines: line 269 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (prompt resolution inside `_resolve_system`), and line 514 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (ISF log summarizer). Neither line is an agentic finder reading real codebase modules. The actual agentic code-grounding path flows through the workflow engine's `RunnerAgentStep` (not exposed at these lines), so the cited evidence for the architectural claim is wrong.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules' as the mechanism by which class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding is code-real. But line 269 of passes.py is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_resolve_system` (prompt library lookup), and line 514 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `summarize_for_isf` (also a prompt lookup). Neither line is an agentic code-grounding finder. The `repo_root` argument at those lines resolves prompt-override files from `.rebar/prompts/`, not the codebase for capability checking. An implementer auditing the mechanism against these citations would find a mismatch.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: '(b) code-grounding = the real repo: … the DET-floor absence probe (det_floor.py:345,392) and the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules`' with '`review_plan(…, repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` sets PlanContext.repo_root (orchestrator.py:195-203) and cfg.repo_path, which the AGENT-tier criteria (G1G2, E4, A1, G6 — registry.py:66) use for code grounding; det_floor.py:345,392 use ctx.repo_root for the DET-floor absence probes.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
